channel.title: DutchNews.nl
channel.description: DutchNews.nl brings daily news from The Netherlands in English

item.title: Amsterdam police chief pledges safety ahead of WorldPride
item.description:
Amsterdam police chief pledges safety ahead of WorldPride

July 23, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Amsterdam police chief Peter Holla opened the COC’s Shakespeare Club at the H’ART Museum on Wednesday, telling dozens of drag queens and drag kings that behind the high heels and the glamour lies a great deal of courage – courage which, he said, “deserves admiration” and “deserves safety”.

The performers arrived by boat from the nearby Stopera, the city hall and opera house complex, at the museum’s jetty on the Amstel, where the ceremony was led by the drag figurehead Dolly Bellefleur.

The Shakespeare Club is a temporary community space run by the LGBTQI+ rights organisation COC Amsterdam. It runs until July 31 and opened three days ahead of WorldPride, the international Pride festival which Amsterdam is hosting for the first time from July 25 to August 8.

“We are a city of tolerance, but it is hard work to actually make that true,” Holla said in his speech, according to the Amsterdam broadcaster AT5. “That affects me and it affects us as Amsterdam police.” Some groups have a harder time than others, he said, and a day like this was a chance to show the force was there for them too.

Holla also set out what the police do for the community, including giving information sessions to refugees from countries where officers are, in his words, “a source of fear”. Officers from Roze in Blauw, the national police network for LGBTIQ+ people, join the Canal Parade as well as Asian Pride, the Trans Pride Walk, and the Trans Diner, he said.

He urged anyone facing intimidation or violence to go to the police. “Reporting is not easy, and many victims are afraid or just want to get on with their lives,” Holla said. “But we really need the information.”

Bellefleur said the speech showed what an unusual country the Netherlands is, because that kind of public backing from the police is rare in other nations.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: “The Dutch are still alive and floating despite all this water”
item.description:
“The Dutch are still alive and floating despite all this water”

July 23, 2026 Brandon Hartley

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Originally from Venezuela, Eduardo Petit has lived in the Netherlands for over two decades. He currently works as a legal counsel in Amsterdam, enjoys the city’s beer bars, and would love to have Herman van Veen sing at his wedding.

How did you end up in the Netherlands?

I came over to study in 2004. None of my former bosses could believe that I was making the decision to go to the Netherlands. I still remember one of them asking me, “Are you really going to study law in a non-English-speaking country?” They didn’t understand at all. The Netherlands isn’t a destination for many people from Venezuela.

I couldn’t convince them or any of the other people surrounding me at that moment in my life, but I was convinced it was the right decision. I wanted to encounter a culture that was very different from my own and very attractive to me.

But I considered the Netherlands as my entry to Europe. I had no intention of staying here. Towards the end of my master’s, I thought about moving to Switzerland. I even studied French for it, but that didn’t happen, and here I am over 21 years later.

How do you describe yourself – an expat, lovepat, immigrant, international?

I guess I’m a staypat and a dreamer. A staypat is an expat who comes to another country and stays. I added dreamer because I’ve always dreamt that I’ll leave the Netherlands, but the deadline has never been clear. Perhaps there is a reason for that?

How long do you plan to stay?

I’m not planning to leave. I’ve never had a timeline, except at the beginning. Back then, I thought I would stay a few years. Now all these years later, there is no deadline. Who knows when I’ll actually leave? I guess time is wise and will indicate the path, but I don’t see myself staying here until I retire. I think I would rather go to a warmer place, which is my idea with my girlfriend.

Do you speak Dutch and how did you learn? 

I do, but I think that learning Dutch, especially if you live in a big city like Amsterdam, is a never-ending story. There’s still so much to learn, even if you live here for many years, because there are so many different ways to say things. There are street words and slang. That’s been my experience.

I took courses, but my best learning method was my personal relationships. I had a long relationship with a Dutch girl. If there’s one thing I have to be thankful for from that relationship, it’s that she was patient with me and with this process of learning. Her and her family really helped; just sitting there at the table with them and listening. After one or two years, I started to understand what was going on.

What’s your favourite Dutch thing?

Biking. You can use a bike for everything here, even dating. You can ride a bike to the place, have the date, and then use the bike to get back. That’s pretty much my favourite Dutch thing, the ease of being able to pretty much live your whole life on a bike.

How Dutch have you become?

I’m now 46. That means I’ve spent about half of my life in this country. I tend to say 30% of me is Dutch. Why not more? Because there’s still a big portion of me that is international and that is Latin. I see and feel things differently. I see myself differently than the locals do.

As for the 30% that’s Dutch, I’m way more balanced with my views. I’m more concise and right to the point when it comes to business and virtually any other type of subject. I like to get to the point and there’s not much of that in the Latin world. In the Latin world, it’s more about storytelling. That’s what the conversations are like there.

People tell me I’m more Dutch, even my own family and Latin friends. I now really like planning. I’m not obsessed, but I like having a plan. It still eats at me sometimes because I love the spontaneous Latin way of life, but this is just how things work here, even on my weekends. Why do I have to plan everything for a weekend?

Which three Dutch people (dead or alive) would you most like to meet?

Willem Drees. He was a politician and the prime minister of the Netherlands after World War II. The most important thing about him was the reconstruction of the country after the war and his social policies. He was a social democrat and one of his policies was to pool money and create the pension system.

He had a pragmatic view and realised that not everything was possible. He knew we could dream of a beautiful ideological society, but it could only be built slowly and step by step. I think his views and the way he went about his policies made him a very interesting man.

Herman van Veen. His voice made me believe that singing in Dutch can be beautiful. I never thought that was going to be possible, but then I heard his music. Another reason I would like to meet him is to invite him to sing at my wedding.

Johan Cruyff. He was a great footballer and a businessman. He helped build up the Barcelona club, but there’s also all the things he said. They were very simple and funny. I wish I could meet with him, have a beer, and just listen to him and laugh.

What’s your top tourist tip?

When I have friends visiting, I sometimes like to take them to the beer bars, but not the ones everyone knows about. I take them to the ones I call the hidden jewels, the small ones where there aren’t too many tourists.

I often tell people to get lost in Amsterdam or at least get out of the Red Light District. Go to Amsterdam-Noord, for example. It’s booming and there’s a lot going on there. There’s NDSM, the street art museum, and all the graffitti. It’s all amazing and different from other parts of the city. It has a contemporary landscape.

Tell us something surprising you’ve found out about the Netherlands.

How the Dutch are still alive despite all this water and how they’ve kept floating after so many years. That’s something I admire. It all works because, here we are, especially with the old houses. I live in one of them in the centre of Amsterdam. For me, it’s amazing that we can live here with all this water.

If you had just 24 hours left in the Netherlands, what would you do?

I would go to the places where something interesting happened to me over these past 21 years. I would go check on the cinema I love the most, the Eye Film Museum. It’s beautiful and I could watch the sunset from there.

I would also want to go on a beer tour to all the places where I have great memories. In De Wildeman is one I would put on the list. Café In ’t Aepjen is another and it has amazing stories and history. I would also want to get some herring. I like herring, funnily enough.

Eduardo was talk to Brandon Hartley. You can learn more about Bucare Consultancy, Eduardo’s consultancy firm, on its website. You can also connect with him via his LinkedIn profile.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Hartley

item.title: Rising costs force Amsterdam’s Kwaku festival to change
item.description:
Rising costs force Amsterdam’s Kwaku festival to change

July 23, 2026 Lauren Comiteau

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Due to rising costs and regulatory pressure, this year’s Amsterdam Kwaku Summer Festival will be the last in its current form, its organizers have said.

“Kwaku is more than a festival – it is a unique social phenomenon that has traditionally meant a great deal to many people,” the organisation said in a press release, describing the move as a difficult decision.

“Stricter permit conditions, rising costs and increasing organisational complexity make it increasingly difficult to keep the festival affordable and accessible for everyone for whom it is actually intended. The organisation feels compelled to acknowledge that the balance in the current setup can no longer be maintained.”

“A shame”

“It’s a shame,” Zuidoost resident Alexandar Petrovic told DutchNews. “It’s a nice, big festival with great music and good times. There’s no money for that, but there is to buy F-35 fighter jets from the American military?”

Initially started as a football tournament in 1975 in the multicultural Bijlmer neighbourhood of the city’s Zuidoost, Kwaku grew into a festival with more than 120,000 visitors annually.

This year’s edition will go ahead as planned at Amsterdam’s Nelson Mandela Park in the weekends up until August 1 and 2. Featured acts include En Vogue, Kassav, Broederliefde and Dutch jazz legend Trijntje Oosterhuis. 

Kwaku is consulting with local city officials to see if it’s possible to hold a different version of the festival in the coming years instead of closing down completely. Other festivals in the country are also struggling to stay afloat for similar reasons.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Comiteau

item.title: Unproven policy means less care for young women in crisis
item.description:
Unproven policy means less care for young women in crisis

July 23, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

A growing group of young women with severe mental illness are getting stuck in the Dutch mental health system, and several clinics are responding by deliberately treating them less, an investigation by current affairs programme Nieuwsuur has found.

The women, estimated to number in the hundreds and aged between 15 and 30, have serious problems including chronic suicidal thoughts, self-harm and eating disorders, for which existing treatments are not working well enough.

Many are of average or above-average intelligence and struggle with perfectionism and a fear of failing or of not meeting social expectations, said Daan Creemers, a professor specialising in suicidality in adolescents.

When no treatment helps, several institutions turn to an approach known as Autonomie Bevorderend Beleid, or autonomy-promoting policy, under which women who ask for help do not always get it – including when they ask to be admitted. The aim is to break a cycle of repeated crises and admissions and give patients back control over their own lives.

“At a certain point you end up in a pattern in which we, as clinicians, feel held hostage,” Creemers told the programme, saying the policy is intended to return responsibility for young people’s safety to them.

There is no guideline for the approach and no scientific evidence that it works. The patients’ organisation MIND raised the alarm 18 months ago after receiving more than 100 negative accounts from people who said they felt neglected, and reported that in many cases the policy did not reduce suicidal thoughts and sometimes made them worse.

The parents of a 24-year-old woman from Eindhoven who took her own life in 2024, hours after being refused help at a clinic under the policy, have begun disciplinary proceedings. The case has not yet been decided.

MIND says it is not against the policy in principle, but that it should not be used until it is properly applied. The organisation has written to the health ministry calling for urgent research into the group. Creemers said the approach requires stable teams and the same clinicians over long periods, which staff turnover makes difficult.

If you or someone close to you is struggling with thoughts of suicide, help is available. In the Netherlands, call 113 or 0800-0113 (free) for 24/7 support, or visit 113.nl, where the chat service is available in English.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Highly contagious impetigo infection on the rise among children
item.description:
Highly contagious impetigo infection on the rise among children

July 23, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

The highly contagious skin infection impetigo is on the rise on among Dutch children, with 2026 seeing more cases than in previous years.

For every 100,000 children, there have been a reported 350 to 400 children with the infection in the past three weeks compared to below 300 cases in previous years, according to research institute Nivel and based on data from GPs.

The infection, known as krentenbaard (currant beard) in Dutch, is mostly seen around the nose and mouth of young children, who develop red spots, bumps and blisters with a puss-like fluid that forms a crust when scratched.

The national public health institute RIVM advises parents to prevent their children from scratching the itchy and burning blisters and to have them routinely wash their hands with soap and water.

Impetigo, which is more common in the warm summer months, is caused by bacteria but can be treated with an antibiotic ointment. But because many people don’t seek medical help for its mild symptoms that often heal on their own, it’s difficult to assess how often it occurs.

According to Nivel, this year’s peaks coincide with the higher temperatures reported by the national weather institute KNMI.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Comiteau

item.title: Summery weather until Sunday, up to 29° in the southeast
item.description:
Summery weather until Sunday, up to 29° in the southeast

July 23, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

The Netherlands is in for a run of summery weather, with temperatures climbing towards the weekend before showers arrive on Sunday, weather service Weeronline said.

Thursday will bring clouds with the odd light shower, though most places will stay dry, and the clouds should break up in the afternoon to give some sunshine. Maximum temperatures will be between 18° and 24°.

Saturday will be the warmest day, staying dry for much of the time with sunny spells. A moderate west to northwesterly wind will keep the coast cooler, at 20° to 24°, while inland reaches 25° to 28° and the southeast up to 29°. There is a chance of a shower on the northern coast in the afternoon and evening.

Showers are expected more widely on Sunday, mainly in the afternoon, with temperatures back to between 20° and 24°. Next week brings a mix of showers and dry, sunny spells, with Monday between 19° and 24°.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Ali B cancels talk amid fears of media spectacle
item.description:
Ali B cancels talk amid fears of media spectacle

July 23, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Rapper and convicted rapist Ali B has pulled out of a business event where he was supposed to be speaking on Thursday in Vinkeveen. The Missing Pieces entrepreneurial event has also been postponed until a later date.

“Due to the exceptionally large number of media applications for the Missing Pieces event, the evening is in danger of becoming a media spectacle. That is at odds with what this meeting is meant for,” reads a joint statement by the rapper and Missing Pieces on the latter’s website. “Therefore, we [Missing Pieces and Ali] have jointly decided to move the event.”

Ali B, or Ali Bouali, was booked, said the website, to speak about “why most entrepreneurs do not break through, despite their talent”.

But following Ali B’s three-year sentence for two rapes in May, including the 2014 rape of Dutch singer Ellen ten Damme, the resulting media interest made the event untenable, Missing Pieces said.

“The whole point was for the evening to be about entrepreneurship, without his personal situation being up for discussion,” reads the joint statement. “Due to the scale of the media attention, it is not feasible at this time.”

The activist group Dolle Mina had also announced a protest at The Harbour Club in Vinkeveen, where the event was scheduled to take place.

The 44-year-old rapper, who has denied all the charges, has appealed to the Supreme Court against the verdict – the highest form of appeal. Ali B has remained free for the years it’s taken his case to wend its way through the courts and will remain so while his appeal is ongoing.

Ticket holders will be able to attend the rescheduled event or get their money back.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Comiteau

item.title: Erasmus Bridge reopens after Rotterdam tram collision
item.description:
Erasmus Bridge reopens after Rotterdam tram collision

July 23, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

The Erasmus Bridge in Rotterdam reopened to all traffic on Wednesday evening after the two badly damaged trams which collided on it that morning were lifted clear of the tracks, broadcaster NOS reported. Trams are running over the bridge again.

Fifteen people were injured, one of them seriously, when a moving tram ran into the back of a stationary one waiting for the bridge to close, regional broadcaster Rijnmond reported.

The salvage operation took most of the day, with the trams having to be cut apart, RET director Linda Boot told the broadcaster.

Three investigations are now under way. Police are examining the crash, including with a drone team, transport company RET is carrying out its own inquiry, and the Dutch Safety Board has begun a preliminary investigation to decide whether to open a full one.

RET said initial checks showed the driver of the rear tram had not been under the influence of alcohol or drugs. He was among those taken to hospital. Boot said it was too early to speculate about the cause.

Rotterdam mayor Carola Schouten visited the scene and called it “a terrible accident”. No further information has been released about the condition of those hurt.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Sixteen councils still without a coalition, four months on
item.description:
Sixteen councils still without a coalition, four months on

July 23, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Sixteen Dutch councils still have not formed a government coalition 126 days after the local elections on March 18 – the highest number ever recorded, according to local government news site Binnenlands Bestuur.

The summer recess will add another 30 days, meaning coalition talks in those councils will have run for more than 150 days. The average formation now stands at 87 days, up from 83 in 2022, and may rise to 90. By comparison, only four councils were still negotiating after the summer in 2022, and in 2018 just one was.

Binnenlands Bestuur points to three reasons. Coalition-building has become more formal, with councils now holding public debates on the election result and appointing scouts and informateurs, where the process was once settled by senior figures behind closed doors.

Councils have also become more fragmented, so a majority often requires four parties or more. And the shift towards right-wing and far-right parties has made it harder to assemble a stable majority, the site said, while vetting the background of candidates for alderman takes longer than it used to.

The outgoing executive stays in place until a new one is appointed, so no council is left without an administration. But caretaker administrations tend to avoid major or politically contested decisions, so new policy largely waits.

Leiden is the only large city still without a coalition, after talks collapsed last month over a lack of trust between PRO and D66. Of the 324 councils that have completed the process, 182 took longer than in 2022, while 115 were quicker – among them The Hague, which did not have a coalition until late September last time. The record is held by Vlieland, which needed 229 days in 2022.
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton

item.title: Half a million Dutch households in energy poverty
item.description:
Half a million Dutch households in energy poverty

July 23, 2026

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

See more DutchNews articles in your Google search results

Add as a favourite source on Google Add DutchNews as a favourite source on Google

Around 503,000 Dutch households were living in energy poverty last year, or 6% of the total – unchanged from 2024, according to figures from statistics office CBS and research institute TNO published on Thursday.

The share had dropped to under 4% in 2023 while government compensation for high energy bills was in place, then jumped back once the support ended. It remains below the 2019 level of nearly 10%.

Researchers looked for the first time at how far people fall short, and found households on low incomes with high energy bills were on average €624 a year short of escaping energy poverty, up from €577 in 2024. Lifting them all out would have cost around €256 million.

Average monthly energy costs rose from €167 to €181 across all households, and from €177 to €188 for those in energy poverty, who now spend 11.5% of their income on energy – more than twice the national average.

The number of low-income households facing high energy bills rose from 385,000 to 410,000, while the number living in poorly insulated homes continued to fall.

Energy poverty is most common in the big cities, north-east Groningen and south Limburg. The deepest shortfalls are in homes with very poor energy efficiency (€1,295 a year) and among owner-occupiers (€967).
item.dc:creator: Louis Gore-Langton
